summary_one_line: Input must contain an entity reference whose name begins with byte 'q' (0x71) so the switch dispatches to case 'q'.

hypotheses.what_input_feature: The input must parse as XML containing an entity reference of the form &q...; so xmlGetPredefinedEntity is called with a name whose first byte equals 'q' (0x71), causing the switch(name[0]) at entities.c:313 to dispatch to the 'q' arm.
hypotheses.why_winner_satisfies: Winner seeds (cmplog) contain the literal byte sequence '&q;' / '&q...' inside their XML body (e.g. Seed 1 has '&q;*' at offset 0x20), so when the parser reaches xmlParseEntityRef the resolved entity name starts with 'q' and the case 'q' arm fires (W shows hits T=168 F=165 on line 313 while L shows T=0 F=98).
hypotheses.why_loser_doesnt: Loser seeds (naive) reach xmlGetPredefinedEntity (entry-line hit 98 times in L) but every call has name[0] != 'q' — their entity references use other letters (e.g. '&TTLI;' in Seed 3, '&...' patterns elsewhere) so case 'q' is never taken (L shows T=0 on line 313).
hypotheses.mechanism_attribution: cmplog's I2SRandReplace observes the byte-wide CMP between name[0] and the constant 'q' inside the switch jump table, captures 'q' as a CMP operand, then substitutes 'q' into the input at offsets matching name[0]. Naive's havoc/token stack has no such CMP-operand source so reaching the exact byte 'q' as the first character of an entity name is left to chance.

evidence_trail.claim: The blocker is the case 'q' arm of switch(name[0]) at entities.c:313, with the true branch globally blocked.
evidence_trail.cited_locator: Location + Source line + Globally blocked side

evidence_trail.claim: The blocked branch is the true side of case 'q'.
cmplog resolves this branch in 10/10 trials while naive blocks in 10/10, with prob_div=1.00.
Naive blocks the branch in every trial.
Winner-side coverage hits case 'q' line many times while loser-side never takes it.
evidence_trail.exact_quote: d=1   L 313  T=168 F=165  T=0 F=98  case 'q':  <-- BLOCKER

evidence_trail.claim: Both winner and loser reach xmlGetPredefinedEntity (entry covered in both, but only winner takes the case 'q' arm).
Winner seed 1 contains the literal entity reference '&q;' which forces name[0]='q' inside xmlGetPredefinedEntity.
A loser seed reaches the entity machinery but its entity references use names that do not start with 'q' (e.g. 'TTLI').
cmplog's mutator stack includes I2SRandReplace, which substitutes CMP operands into the input.
naive has no I2S substitution stage available.
evidence_trail.exact_quote: **Observed `mutation_op` in seed metadata**: any of the above. No I2S.

mechanism_consistency_check.verification_method: Ran `python3 tools/db_query.py lineage --branch 2274 --role W --fuzzer cmplog --trial 1 --seed 6ccd943329cab99a`. Chain has 6 ancestor levels; depth 5 ancestor (8c7bd7ba6dfb824c) is an I2S-floor row (mutation_op = -, ParentInfo-only metadata). Per fuzzer_mechanism_library.md cmplog section, dash rows are exclusive to cmplog/vpc under the current LibAFL build and indicate an unwrapped I2SRandReplace find (lower bound — some I2S finds leak into the havoc bucket via wrapped sub-mutator tagging).

falsifiability.would_be_refuted_by: If a synthetic harness exposing the switch(name[0]) over predefined-entity letters is solved by naive within a comparable wall-clock budget (i.e. naive reaches the 'q' arm in a meaningful fraction of trials despite lacking any CMP-operand source for the byte 'q'), then I2S-byte substitution is not the bottleneck and the dispatch is reachable by random first-byte mutation alone.

weakest_evidence_point: The lineage floor signal is a lower bound (one dash row at depth 5) that confirms I2S contributed somewhere in the chain, but the immediate leaf mutation list is all-havoc — so we cannot prove the exact byte 'q' that unlocked case 'q' was placed by I2SRandReplace as opposed to landing during subsequent havoc on an I2S-seeded ancestor.
